---
title: "Impact of Outbreak Response Immunization timing on measles outbreaks"
subtitle: "Julia implementation using Starsim.jl"
author: "Simon Frost"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    toc: true
  gfm:
    toc: true
engine: julia
---

## Overview

This vignette reproduces the core analysis from [Delport et al. (2025)](https://gh.bmj.com/content/10/7/e016887), which estimates the impact of decreasing the response time of vaccination campaigns on measles outbreaks. We implement the model on top of `Starsim.jl` using the built-in `SEIR` disease and a custom `FunctionIntervention` for outbreak detection and response.

The model captures:

- A random contact network with mean degree ≈ 3.5
- SEIR dynamics with mortality
- Symptomatic testing that triggers outbreak detection on the first true positive
- Outbreak Response Immunization (ORI) starting a configurable number of days after detection, with a fixed daily dose budget and a 10-day linear ramp to peak vaccine efficacy
- A scenario sweep over response times of 15, 30, 60, 90 and 120 days, plus a no-response baseline

The key finding from Delport et al. is reproduced: shorter ORI response times avert substantially more cases. A matching Python companion (`Python/18_ori.qmd`) runs the same scenarios in Python `starsim`.

## Model parameters

```{julia}
using Starsim
using Statistics
using Random
using Plots
using StableRNGs

const N_AGENTS        = 10_000
const INIT_INFECTED   = 5
const SIM_DAYS        = 365.0
const BETA            = 0.08          # per-contact daily transmission probability
const N_CONTACTS      = 3.5           # mean Poisson contacts per day (RandomNet uses the mean)
const DUR_EXP         = 10.5          # mean latent period (days), original Uniform(9, 12)
const DUR_INF         = 8.0           # mean infectious period (days), original Normal(8, 1)
const P_DEATH         = 0.005
const TEST_PROB       = 0.03          # daily probability of testing while infectious
const TEST_SENS       = 0.87
const VAX_EFFICACY    = 0.83
const VAX_RAMP_DAYS   = 10.0
const DAILY_DOSES     = 100
```

## Outbreak response intervention

`FunctionIntervention` is the simplest way to wire bespoke per-step logic into a `Sim`. The closure below maintains its own state (diagnosed agents, detection day, vaccination queue, time-of-vaccination per agent) and updates `rel_sus` each day to model the linear ramp to peak vaccine efficacy. Pass `response_time = nothing` to disable ORI.

```{julia}
function build_ori_intervention(; response_time, n_agents, seed)
    rng = StableRNG(seed)
    diagnosed     = falses(n_agents)
    ti_vacc       = fill(NaN, n_agents)
    detected      = Ref(false)
    detection_day = Ref(0.0)
    queue         = Int[]
    queue_pos     = Ref(0)

    fn = function (sim, ti)
        seir         = sim.diseases[:seir]
        infected     = seir.infection.infected.raw
        susceptible  = seir.infection.susceptible.raw
        rel_sus      = seir.infection.rel_sus.raw
        day          = Float64(ti)
        n            = length(infected)

        # 1. Symptomatic testing of currently infectious agents
        for i in 1:n
            if infected[i] && !diagnosed[i]
                if rand(rng) < TEST_PROB && rand(rng) < TEST_SENS
                    diagnosed[i] = true
                end
            end
        end

        # 2. First positive triggers outbreak detection and (if requested) builds the vaccination queue
        if !detected[] && any(diagnosed)
            detected[]      = true
            detection_day[] = day
            if response_time !== nothing
                susceptible_uids = findall(susceptible)
                shuffle!(rng, susceptible_uids)
                append!(queue, susceptible_uids)
            end
        end

        # 3. After the configured delay, vaccinate up to DAILY_DOSES susceptibles per day
        if detected[] && response_time !== nothing && day >= detection_day[] + response_time
            doses = 0
            while doses < DAILY_DOSES && queue_pos[] < length(queue)
                queue_pos[] += 1
                u = queue[queue_pos[]]
                if susceptible[u] && isnan(ti_vacc[u])
                    ti_vacc[u] = day
                    doses += 1
                end
            end
        end

        # 4. Update vaccine immunity: linear ramp from 0 to VAX_EFFICACY over VAX_RAMP_DAYS
        for i in 1:n
            tv = ti_vacc[i]
            if !isnan(tv) && susceptible[i]
                ramp = clamp((day - tv) / VAX_RAMP_DAYS, 0.0, 1.0)
                rel_sus[i] = 1.0 - VAX_EFFICACY * ramp
            end
        end
        return nothing
    end

    return FunctionIntervention(name=:ori, fn=fn)
end
```

## Building a sim

```{julia}
function make_sim(; response_time, seed, stop = SIM_DAYS, n_agents = N_AGENTS)
    iv = build_ori_intervention(; response_time, n_agents, seed)
    return Sim(
        n_agents      = n_agents,
        networks      = RandomNet(n_contacts = N_CONTACTS),
        diseases      = SEIR(
            beta      = BETA,
            dur_exp   = DUR_EXP,
            dur_inf   = DUR_INF,
            p_death   = P_DEATH,
            init_prev = INIT_INFECTED / n_agents,
        ),
        interventions = iv,
        dt            = 1.0,
        start         = 0.0,
        stop          = stop,
        rand_seed     = seed,
        verbose       = 0,
    )
end
```

## Single illustrative runs

```{julia}
sim_baseline = make_sim(response_time = nothing, seed = 1)
run!(sim_baseline)

sim_fast = make_sim(response_time = 15.0, seed = 1)
run!(sim_fast)

sim_slow = make_sim(response_time = 90.0, seed = 1)
run!(sim_slow)

cum_inf(sim) = sum(get_result(sim, :seir, :new_infections))

println("Cumulative infections (single run, seed = 1):")
println("  No ORI:           ", round(Int, cum_inf(sim_baseline)))
println("  ORI at 15 days:   ", round(Int, cum_inf(sim_fast)))
println("  ORI at 90 days:   ", round(Int, cum_inf(sim_slow)))
```

## Scenario sweep

We run a modest number of stochastic replicates per scenario so the vignette renders quickly. Increase `n_seeds` for tighter intervals.

```{julia}
function run_scenarios(response_times; n_seeds = 25)
    results = Dict{Any, Vector{Float64}}()
    for rt in response_times
        finals = Float64[]
        for s in 1:n_seeds
            sim = make_sim(response_time = rt, seed = s)
            run!(sim)
            push!(finals, cum_inf(sim))
        end
        results[rt] = finals
    end
    return results
end

response_times = [nothing, 15.0, 30.0, 60.0, 90.0, 120.0]
scenario_results = run_scenarios(response_times; n_seeds = 25)

println("Scenario           Mean cum. infections   (sd)")
for rt in response_times
    label = rt === nothing ? "No ORI" : "ORI @ $(Int(rt)) days"
    vals  = scenario_results[rt]
    println(rpad(label, 18), lpad(round(Int, mean(vals)), 10),
            "   (", round(std(vals), digits = 1), ")")
end
```

```{julia}
labels = [rt === nothing ? "No ORI" : "$(Int(rt))" for rt in response_times]
means  = [mean(scenario_results[rt]) for rt in response_times]
sds    = [std(scenario_results[rt])  for rt in response_times]

bar(labels, means;
    yerr = sds,
    legend = false,
    xlabel = "ORI response time (days)",
    ylabel = "Cumulative infections",
    title  = "Impact of ORI response timing on measles outbreak size")
```

## Epidemic curves

A small ensemble of incidence trajectories per scenario.

```{julia}
function ensemble_curves(rt; n_seeds = 10)
    curves = Vector{Vector{Float64}}()
    for s in 1:n_seeds
        sim = make_sim(response_time = rt, seed = s)
        run!(sim)
        push!(curves, copy(get_result(sim, :seir, :new_infections)))
    end
    return curves
end

scenarios_to_plot = [(nothing, "No ORI"), (15.0, "15 days"), (60.0, "60 days"), (120.0, "120 days")]

plt = plot(xlabel = "Day", ylabel = "New infections", title = "Daily incidence by ORI response time")
for (rt, label) in scenarios_to_plot
    curves = ensemble_curves(rt; n_seeds = 10)
    mean_curve = mean(curves)
    plot!(plt, mean_curve, label = label, linewidth = 2)
end
plt
```

## Discussion

The Starsim.jl version replaces the hand-rolled SEIR + ORI loop with composable framework primitives:

- `SEIR` provides the disease dynamics, exposed/infectious/recovered/dead transitions and result tracking
- `RandomNet` provides the daily contact structure
- A single `FunctionIntervention` carries per-step state for symptomatic testing, outbreak detection, ORI scheduling and the vaccine ramp

This makes it easy to swap in alternative networks (e.g. clustered or sexual networks), add a routine vaccination programme via `routine_vx`, or run the same scenarios on GPU with a different array backend. The Python companion in `Python/18_ori.qmd` mirrors the structure using Python `starsim`. Some simplifications relative to the original Delport et al. ABM (fixed-mean exposed/infectious durations rather than per-agent draws from `Uniform`/`Normal`, fixed integer contact degree rather than per-agent Poisson) keep the implementation concise; the qualitative ORI-timing effect is preserved.
